43	
44	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker
45	
46	You compare **one Lean file** against **its blueprint chapter** and report whether the Lean follows the blueprint faithfully. You are read-only.
47	
48	## Your Job
49	
50	Your directive names one `.lean` file (e.g. `Algebra/WLocal.lean`) and its corresponding blueprint chapter (e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex`). You audit **bidirectionally**:
51	
52	### (A) Does the Lean follow the blueprint?
53	
54	For each `\lean{...}`-tagged block in the chapter, verify the Lean side:
55	
56	- **Declaration exists** with the right name (matches `\lean{namespace.theorem_name}`).
57	- **Signature matches** the informal statement in the chapter — the types are what the prose says they should be. A statement that type-checks but uses the wrong type class (e.g. `Smooth f` where the prose says "smooth of relative dimension n") is **wrong**, not "approximate".
58	- **Proof follows the blueprint sketch** — the proof in the Lean file should be a formalization of the steps in the chapter's `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}`, not a different argument that happens to produce the same conclusion.
59	- **No fake / placeholder statements** — definitions or theorems whose body is just `:= sorry` or a trivially-true tautology while the chapter prose claims something substantive.
60	- **No "temporary" definitions left in place** — comments like `-- TODO: replace with the real definition`, `-- placeholder until we figure out X`, or definitions whose body is suspicious (e.g. `def Foo := True`, weakened-wrong definitions standing in for the real concept).
61	- **No comments that explain away wrong code** — "we are using a wrong definition for now", "this proof is incomplete but will be fixed", etc. These are red flags, not acceptable workflow notes.
62	
63	### (B) Is the blueprint adequate for this Lean file?
64	
65	The blueprint can also be the failure mode. For the chapter you're reading, check:
66	
67	- **Every Lean declaration in this file should map to a blueprint block** (via `\lean{...}`) OR be an explicitly-helper-only declaration. If the file has substantive declarations the blueprint doesn't reference at all, the chapter is incomplete.
68	- **Proof sketches in the blueprint should be detailed enough** for a prover to formalize correctly. If you see Lean proofs that clearly went through significant reasoning the blueprint's `\begin{proof}` does NOT preview, flag the chapter as under-specified.
69	- **`\lean{...}` hints should pin the Lean target precisely.** If the blueprint says "smooth morphism" without specifying which Mathlib predicate (`Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n` vs `IsSmooth`), and the Lean file ended up with the wrong one, that's BOTH a Lean error AND a blueprint clarity failure. Flag both sides.
70	- **Definitions in the blueprint should be at the right level of generality** for the Lean to consume. If the blueprint defines something narrower than the project actually needs and the Lean had to write a parallel API to cover the gap, that's a blueprint adequacy failure.
71	
72	Both directions matter. Don't excuse a wrong Lean statement by saying "the blueprint is loose"; don't excuse a thin blueprint by saying "the Lean works anyway." Report both.
73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker Directive
78	
79	## Slug
80	<slug>
81	
82	## Lean file
83	<path/to/file.lean>
84	
85	## Blueprint chapter
86	blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex
87	
88	## Known issues
89	<things the review agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
90	```
91	
92	That's it. No strategy snapshot, no references, no full project context — just the two files and any pre-known issues to skip. Cross-file consistency is the job of the code-audit and blueprint-review subagents in the catalog; you stay focused on this one pair.
93	
94	## What you do
95	
96	1. Read your directive.
97	2. Read the Lean file completely. Note every declaration (`def`, `theorem`, `lemma`, `instance`, `axiom`, …) and its signature.
98	3. Read the blueprint chapter completely. List every `\lean{...}` reference and its corresponding declaration block.
99	4. **For each `\lean{...}` block**: locate the Lean declaration. Verify:
100	   - It exists.
101	   - Its signature matches the informal statement (use `archon-lean-lsp` for hover/signature info).
102	   - If the Lean has a body (proof or definition body), does it match the blueprint's prose? Naming and tactic choice can differ; the *mathematical content* should match.
103	5. **For each declaration in the Lean file**: is it `\lean{...}`-referenced from the blueprint? Unreferenced declarations are notable but not necessarily wrong (helpers may exist). Flag suspect ones.
104	6. **Scan for red flags** in the Lean file:
105	   - `:= sorry` where the blueprint claims a substantive statement (placeholder).
106	   - Suspect bodies like `:= True`, `:= rfl` for non-trivial claims, `:= Classical.choice _`.
107	   - Comments excusing wrong/incomplete code: `-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- temporary`, `-- will fix later`, `-- this is wrong but works for now`.
108	   - `axiom` declarations.
109	7. **Cross-check** any `import` paths and namespace usage that should match what the blueprint's `\lean{...}` hints suggest.
110	
111	You may use:
112	- `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools (read-only): hover, signature lookup, diagnostics.
113	- `sorry_analyzer` if available, to count sorries in the file.
114	
115	You may NOT modify the Lean file, the blueprint, or any other project file. Even when a fix is obvious.
116	
117	## Report format
118	
119	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/lean-vs-blueprint-checker-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
120	
121	```markdown
122	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Check Report
123	
124	## Slug
125	<slug>
126	
127	## Iteration
128	<NNN>
129	
130	## Files audited
131	- Lean: <path>
132	- Blueprint: <path>
133	
134	## Per-declaration
135	
136	For every `\lean{...}` block in the chapter, one entry:
137	
138	### `\lean{Foo.bar}` (chapter: \thm:bar)
139	- **Lean target exists**: yes / no (if no: declaration name in the .lean that comes closest, or "none found")
140	- **Signature matches**: yes / partial / no (with one line on the mismatch)
141	- **Proof follows sketch**: yes / partial / no / N/A (if N/A: chapter doesn't have a proof body to compare against)
142	- **notes**: <free-form, one line per observation>
143	
144	### `\lean{Foo.baz}` ...
145	
146	## Red flags
147	
148	(Section appears only if there are findings.)
149	
150	### Placeholder / suspect bodies
151	- `Foo.bar` at line 42: body is `:= sorry` but blueprint claims a substantive proof.
152	- `Foo.qux` at line 78: defined as `:= True`, suspect.
153	- ...
154	
155	### Excuse-comments
156	- `Foo.lean:113`: comment "TODO: replace with real def" attached to `Foo.frob`. Blueprint claims `Foo.frob` is the actual definition, not a placeholder.
157	- ...
158	
159	### Axioms / Classical.choice on non-trivial claims
160	- `Foo.lean:200`: `axiom` declaration `Foo.skip_step` introduced. Blueprint does not authorize this.
161	
162	## Unreferenced declarations (informational)
163	
164	<list of declarations in the Lean file that no `\lean{...}` references. Most are helpers — only flag the ones whose name suggests they should be in the blueprint.>
165	
166	## Blueprint adequacy for this file
167	
168	A bidirectional check: does the blueprint chapter give a prover enough detail to formalize this file correctly?
169	
170	- **Coverage**: <N>/<M> Lean declarations have a corresponding `\lean{...}` block in the chapter. Unreferenced declarations: <count> helpers (acceptable) + <count> substantive (flagged below).
171	- **Proof-sketch depth**: <one of: adequate / under-specified / silent>. If under-specified or silent, list which `\thm:...` blocks need expansion.
172	- **Hint precision**: <one of: precise / loose / wrong>. Loose hints (the prose pins one Mathlib predicate but the `\lean{...}` doesn't, leaving the prover to guess) get flagged. Wrong hints (the `\lean{...}` names a declaration whose signature doesn't match the prose) are critical.
173	- **Generality**: <one of: matches need / too narrow / too broad>. If too narrow, name the parallel API that the project ended up writing because the blueprint didn't cover the needed level of generality.
174	- **Recommended chapter-side actions**: <bullet list of items a blueprint-writing subagent should land if you flagged anything above>
175	
176	## Severity summary
177	
178	Apply these rules verbatim. Do NOT under-classify to soften the blow — the plan agent's gates depend on accurate severity, and a hidden critical finding hardens into shipped wrong code.
179	
180	- **must-fix-this-iter** — every one of the following lands here, no exceptions:
181	  - Placeholder body (`:= sorry`, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim, suspect `:= Classical.choice _` patterns) on a declaration the blueprint claims is substantive.
182	  - Signature mismatch with the blueprint's prose — including using the wrong Mathlib predicate (e.g. `Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n`) when the prose pins one specific predicate.
183	  - Excuse-comments (`-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- temporary`, `-- placeholder`, `-- wrong but works for now`) on declarations the blueprint claims are real.
184	  - Axioms or `Classical.choice _` on substantive claims the blueprint does NOT authorize.
185	  - Blueprint adequacy failure: chapter is so under-specified that a prover could not have formalized this file correctly from prose alone.
186	  - Weakened-wrong definitions: Lean defines a structurally-different stand-in (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic Γ(X,⊤)` when the blueprint defines it as an invertible quasi-coherent O_X-module).
187	- **major** — partial signature mismatches that are fixable in-place, missing `\lean{...}` references to declarations that the blueprint should reference, comments that are stale but not actively misleading.
188	- **minor** — naming drift, helpers worth promoting to blueprint blocks, low-impact prose-vs-Lean differences.
189	
190	Overall verdict: one sentence.
191	```
192	
193	The plan agent's per-file gate (see plan.md and the blueprint-review subagent's `dispatcher_notes` in the catalog) treats every must-fix-this-iter finding as blocking. If you classify a wrong signature as "major" to avoid the gate firing, you are working against the project, not for it.
194	
195	## Return value
196	
197	Your final assistant message:
198	
199	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> declarations checked, <M> red flags`
200	- The path to your full report.
201	
202	## Reminders
203	
204	- **One file pair, no global context.** Cross-file consistency belongs to the catalog's code-audit and blueprint-review subagents.
205	- **You are read-only.** No project source, no blueprint, no state files (except your own report).
206	- **Excuse-comments are red flags, not workflow.** Treat "we use a wrong def for now" with the suspicion it deserves.
207	- **Mathematical content match, not syntactic.** The Lean proof can choose different tactics than the chapter's sketch implies, as long as the mathematical steps are the same.
208

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

43	\section{Setup and motivation}
44	\label{sec:codim1_setup}
45	
46	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) throughout. By a \emph{variety} over \(\bar k\)
47	we mean a geometrically integral, separated scheme of finite type over \(\bar k\)
48	(Milne's convention, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~v); a variety is \emph{nonsingular}
49	(or \emph{smooth}) if its local ring at every closed point is regular. A variety is
50	\emph{complete} if it is proper over \(\bar k\).
51	
52	A \emph{rational map} \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) between varieties is the equivalence
53	class of a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) defined on a dense open subset
54	\(U \subseteq X\), where two pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\), \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) are equivalent
55	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\) (Milne~\S I.3, p.~16). The map \(f\)
56	is \emph{defined at} a point \(x \in X\) if there is some representative \((U, \varphi_U)\)
57	with \(x \in U\); the set of such \(x\) is an open subset \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \subseteq X\),
58	the \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\).
59	
60	The motivating question of this chapter, following Milne~\S I.3, is:
61	\begin{quote}
62	  \emph{When does a rational map \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) extend to a regular
63	  morphism on all of \(X\)?}
64	\end{quote}
65	Without hypotheses on \(X\) or \(Y\) the question has no positive answer (Milne lists three
66	counterexamples on p.~16: the inclusion of a proper open into the ambient variety; the
67	rational map to the cuspidal cubic whose inverse fails to extend; the inverse of a
68	blow-up at a smooth point). The two structural inputs that buy unconditional extension
69	in our setting are:
70	\begin{itemize}
71	  \item \emph{Target completeness:} if \(Y\) is complete (proper over \(\bar k\)), the
72	    valuative criterion of properness forces the rational map to extend across every
73	    codim-\(1\) point of a normal source \(X\) (Milne Theorem~3.1).
74	  \item \emph{Target group structure:} if \(Y\) is a group variety, the difference map
75	    \(\Phi(x, y) := f(x) \cdot f(y)^{-1}\) on \(X \times X\) has a vanishing/pole divisor
76	    along the diagonal that controls the codimension of the indeterminacy locus
77	    (Milne Lemma~3.3).
78	\end{itemize}
79	For \(Y\) an abelian variety both conditions hold, and combining them yields Milne's
80	Theorem~3.2 (the headline of \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}). The
81	present chapter packages the two inputs separately:
82	\cref{thm:codim_one_extension} is Milne~3.1, and
83	\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} is Milne~3.3. The Weil-divisor reformulation
84	\cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} translates the codim-\(1\) extension obstruction
85	in Milne~3.1's proof into the order-at-a-prime-divisor language pinned in
86	\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, which is the form the Lean prover consumes when
87	working with the project's \(\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}\) API.
88	
89	\section{The indeterminacy locus of a rational map}
90	\label{sec:indeterm_locus}
91	
92	\begin{definition}
93	
94	\leanok
95	  [Indeterminacy locus of a rational map]
96	  \label{def:indeterminacy_locus}
97	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus}
98	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S I.3, p.~16, the paragraph "Let \(V\) and \(W\)
99	  % be varieties over \(k\)\ldots" defining the domain of definition of a rational map
100	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
101	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi\) is said to be \emph{defined} at a point
102	  % \(v\) of \(V\) if \(v \in U\) for some \((U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi\). The set \(U_1\) of
103	  % \(v\) at which \(\varphi\) is defined is open, and there is a regular map
104	  % \(\varphi_1 \colon U_1 \to W\) such that \((U_1, \varphi_1) \in \varphi\) --- clearly,
105	  % \(U_1 = \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi} U\) and we can define \(\varphi_1\) to be
106	  % the regular map such that \(\varphi_1 | U = \varphi_U\) for all $(U, \varphi_U) \in
107	  % \varphi$."
108	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S I.3, p.~16.}
109	  Let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map between varieties over \(\bar k\).
110	  The \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\) is the open set
111	  \[
112	    \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;:=\; \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in f} U \;\subseteq\; X,
113	  \]
114	  i.e.\ the union of the dense opens of all representatives of \(f\). The
115	  \emph{indeterminacy locus} of \(f\) is the closed complement
116	  \[
117	    Z(f) \;:=\; X \smallsetminus \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;\subseteq\; X.
118	  \]
119	  The map \(f\) is regular (i.e.\ defined on all of \(X\)) if and only if \(Z(f) = \emptyset\).
120	
121	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
122	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus} is a method on the
123	  Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure pinned at
124	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}; it returns a
125	  \texttt{Set X} (the underlying carrier of the closed complement of
126	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.domain}, which is already exposed by Mathlib). The
127	  closedness of \(Z(f)\) is a separate \texttt{IsClosed} witness, packaged either as a
128	  field of \texttt{indeterminacyLocus} or as a standalone lemma
129	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.isClosed\_indeterminacyLocus}, whichever is cleaner with
130	  Mathlib's existing API at commit \texttt{b80f227}.
131	\end{definition}
132	
133	\begin{definition}
134	
135	\leanok
136	  [Codimension-1-indeterminacy-free rational maps]
137	  \label{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}
138	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree}
139	  
140	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 --- the conclusion
141	  % "an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)"
142	  % is the standing predicate we abstract (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf,
143	  % PDF page 22).
144	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow W\) from a normal
145	  % variety \(V\) to a complete variety \(W\) is defined on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose
146	  % complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)."
147	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 (the
148	  conclusion).}
149	  Let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map between varieties over \(\bar k\).
150	  We say \(f\) is \emph{codim-1-indeterminacy-free} (or has \emph{codimension-\(\geq 2\)
151	  indeterminacy}) if every irreducible component of the closed subset \(Z(f) \subseteq X\)
152	  has codimension \(\geq 2\) in \(X\). Equivalently, no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in
153	  \(Z(f)\); equivalently, every point \(\eta \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\)
154	  lies in \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\).
155	
156	  \paragraph{Why the abstraction.} Milne's Theorem~3.1 (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}
157	  below) and Lemma~3.3 (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} below) both produce
158	  conclusions about the codimension of \(Z(f)\). Phrasing the codim-\(\geq 2\) condition
159	  as a named predicate keeps the cited statements concise and lets the consumer
160	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} read off the combination
161	  (\(Z(f)\) has codim \(\geq 2\) from \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} \emph{and} \(Z(f)\) is
162	  empty or pure codim 1 from \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} \(\Rightarrow\) \(Z(f)\)
163	  is empty) as a single line.
164	
165	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
166	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree} is a \texttt{Prop}-valued
167	  predicate, encoded via Mathlib's \texttt{Order.coheight} on the specialisation
168	  preorder of \texttt{X.carrier} as in
169	  \cref{def:prime_divisor}: \(f\) is codim-1-indeterminacy-free if and only if for every
170	  \(\eta \in X\) with \(\texttt{Order.coheight\ }\eta = 1\), $\eta \in
171	  \texttt{f.domain}\(. The encoding matches the standing \)\mathrm{Order.coheight}$
172	  convention pinned in \cref{def:prime_divisor}, so prime divisors of \(X\) and the
173	  hypothesis ``no codim-\(1\) indeterminacy'' use the same Mathlib idiom.
174	\end{definition}
175	
176	\section{Smoothness yields a DVR at every codim-1 point}
177	\label{sec:smooth_dvr}
178	
179	The codim-\(1\) extension argument in Milne's proof of Theorem~3.1 hinges on the local
180	ring at the generic point of a prime divisor being a discrete valuation ring (so that
181	the valuative criterion of properness applies). On a normal variety this is the
182	defining property of regularity in codimension one; on a smooth variety it is automatic.
183	
184	The smooth-implies-regular-local-stalk implication itself is the Jacobian-criterion
185	direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}; we package it as a separate sub-lemma so that
186	\cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr} below reads cleanly as ``regular local of dimension
187	\(1\) is a DVR'', with the regularity prerequisite supplied by the sub-lemma.
188	
189	\subsection{Stages 5a/5b: K\"ahler-differentials localisation substrate (iter-193)}
190	\label{subsec:kaehler_localisation_substrate}
191	
192	The Jacobian-criterion direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}
193	(\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring} below) factors on the Lean side
194	through a six-stage chain. Stages~1--2 (smooth \(\Rightarrow\) flat at a
195	stalk; smooth \(\Rightarrow\) admits a standard-smooth presentation
196	locally) are axiom-clean since iter-191; Stages~3--4 (the
197	scheme-to-algebra bridge for standard-smooth presentations; freeness of
198	\(\Omega_{S/R}\) for a standard-smooth algebra \(R \to S\)) are
199	axiom-clean since iter-192. The remaining Stages~5--6 reduce the
200	Stacks~00TT conclusion to a freeness plus rank computation on K\"ahler
201	differentials, transported through localisation at the prime defining
202	the stalk. Iter-193 packaged Stages~5a (transport of freeness) and 5b
203	(matching rank under localisation) as the two standalone helpers below;
204	Stage~6 (the remaining Stacks~00OE smooth-algebra dimension formula
205	plus Stacks~02JK cotangent--K\"ahler-over-a-field bridge) is the open
206	Mathlib gap, decomposed iter-198 into the three named sub-lemmas
207	\cref{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula} (6.A),
208	\cref{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field} (6.B), and
209	\cref{lem:stage6_regular_stalk_assembly} (6.C) in
210	\cref{subsec:stage6_subgap_decomposition} immediately following.
211	
212	\begin{lemma}
213	
214	\leanok
215	  [Stage 5a --- K\"ahler-differentials freeness transports through localisation]
216	  \label{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}
217	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.module_free_kaehlerDifferential_localization}
218	  Let \(R\) and \(S\) be commutative rings equipped with an \(R\)-algebra
219	  structure on \(S\), and assume that the module of K\"ahler differentials
220	  \(\Omega_{S/R}\) is free as an \(S\)-module. Let \(M \subseteq S\) be a
221	  submonoid and let \(S_M\) be a localisation of \(S\) at \(M\), so that
222	  \(R \to S \to S_M\) is a tower of \(R\)-algebras. Then the module of
223	  K\"ahler differentials \(\Omega_{S_M / R}\) is free as an
224	  \(S_M\)-module.
225	\end{lemma}
226	
227	\begin{proof}
228	  The canonical comparison map
229	  \(\Omega_{S/R} \to \Omega_{S_M/R}\) induced by the localisation map
230	  \(S \to S_M\) realises \(\Omega_{S_M/R}\) as the localisation of the
231	  \(S\)-module \(\Omega_{S/R}\) at \(M\); i.e.\ it satisfies Mathlib's
232	  \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} predicate along the underlying ring
233	  localisation. This is Stacks tag \texttt{02JK}, packaged in Mathlib as
234	  \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.isLocalizedModule\_map}. Freeness of a
235	  module is preserved under localisation of modules via Mathlib's
236	  \texttt{Module.free\_of\_isLocalizedModule}; combining the two yields
237	  the conclusion.
238	\end{proof}
239	
240	\begin{lemma}
241	
242	\leanok
243	  [Stage 5b --- K\"ahler-differentials rank under localisation equals
244	  the relative dimension]
245	  \label{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim}
246	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.rank_kaehlerDifferential_localization_eq_relativeDimension}
247	  \uses{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}
248	  With notation as in \cref{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization},
249	  assume in addition that the \(R\)-algebra \(S\) is \emph{standard
250	  smooth of relative dimension \(n\)} (Mathlib's
251	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}\,\(n\)\,\(R\)\,\(S\),
252	  the algebra-side packaging of Stacks tag \texttt{00T7}(2)) and that
253	  the localisation \(S_M\) is non-trivial as a ring. Then the rank of
254	  \(\Omega_{S_M / R}\) over \(S_M\) equals \(n\).
255	\end{lemma}
256	
257	\begin{proof}
258	  Mathlib's
259	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.\linebreak rank\_kaehlerDifferential}
260	  asserts \(\mathrm{rank}_S(\Omega_{S/R}) = n\) under the
261	  standard-smooth hypothesis. The localisation map
262	  \(\Omega_{S/R} \to \Omega_{S_M/R}\) is an
263	  \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} along \(S \to S_M\) (Stacks tag
264	  \texttt{02JK}; cf.\ the proof of
265	  \cref{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}), and Mathlib's
266	  \texttt{Module.lift\_rank\_of\_isLocalizedModule\_of\_free} transports
267	  module rank through an \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} up to
268	  \(\mathrm{Cardinal.lift}\). Since \(n\) is a natural number both
269	  ranks equal \(n\), so
270	  \(\mathrm{rank}_{S_M}(\Omega_{S_M / R}) = n\) as required.
271	\end{proof}
272	
273	\subsection{Stage 6 sub-decomposition: 00OE + 02JK chain (iter-198)}
274	\label{subsec:stage6_subgap_decomposition}
275	
276	Stage 6 is the residual gap inside
277	\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}'s six-stage chain: given Stages
278	1--5b (a standard-smooth presentation of an affine chart
279	\(R \to S\) at a point \(z\), with \(\Omega_{S/R}\) free of rank \(n\)
280	and \(\Omega_{S_\mathfrak q / R}\) free of rank \(n\) on the stalk
281	\(S_\mathfrak q = \mathcal O_{X, z}\)), one must conclude that
282	\(S_\mathfrak q\) is a regular local ring. The Stacks proof of tag
283	\texttt{00TT} ultimately reduces to two ingredients which Mathlib does
284	not yet ship at commit \texttt{b80f227}: (6.A) the smooth-algebra
285	Krull-dimension formula (Stacks tag \texttt{00OE}, in the algebra
286	chapter as Lemma~\texttt{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}),
287	and (6.B) the cotangent / K\"ahler-differential bridge over a field
288	base (Stacks tag \texttt{02JK}, in the algebra chapter as
289	Lemma~\texttt{lemma-differential-seq} plus the separable-residue-field
290	refinement of Lemma~\texttt{lemma-separable-smooth}). The two
291	ingredients combine (6.C) into the regularity conclusion via
292	\(\dim S_\mathfrak q = \mathrm{rank}_{S_\mathfrak q}\Omega_{S_\mathfrak
293	q / R} = \dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\),
294	which is the cotangent-space characterisation of regularity that
295	Mathlib's \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing.iff\_finrank\_cotangentSpace}
296	already exposes. The next three lemmas package the three sub-gaps as
297	named iter-199+ prover targets.
298	
299	\begin{lemma}
300	  [Stage 6.A --- Smooth-algebra Krull-dimension formula (Stacks 00OE)]
301	  \label{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula}
302	  \lean{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.ringKrullDim_localization_eq_relativeDimension}
303	  \uses{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim}
304	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field
305	  % (canonically known as tag 00OE; tag-to-label cross-check not in the
306	  % local `tags/tags` file, see iter-198 writer report note) (read from
307	  % references/stacks-algebra.tex, L28206--28217 in section
308	  % "Dimension theory of finitely generated $k$-algebras").
309	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $k$ be a field. Let $S$ be a finite type $k$
310	  % algebra. Let $X = \Spec(S)$. Let $\mathfrak p \subset S$ be a prime
311	  % ideal, and let $x \in X$ be the corresponding point. Then we have
312	  % $\dim_x(X) = \dim(S_{\mathfrak p}) + \text{trdeg}_k\
313	  % \kappa(\mathfrak p)$."
314	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag~00OE (\(=\) \texttt{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}).}
315	  Let \(k\) be a field, let \(S\) be a finite-type \(k\)-algebra, and let
316	  \(\mathfrak q \subset S\) be a prime ideal corresponding to a point
317	  \(x \in \mathrm{Spec}(S)\). Then
318	  \[
319	    \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S)
320	    \;=\; \dim S_{\mathfrak q} + \mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q).
321	  \]
322	  In particular, if \(\mathfrak q\) is a maximal ideal (so \(x\) is a
323	  closed point) and \(k\) is algebraically closed, then
324	  \(\kappa(\mathfrak q) = k\) (Nullstellensatz), so
325	  \(\mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q) = 0\) and
326	  \(\dim S_{\mathfrak q} = \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S)\).
327	
328	  \paragraph{Geometric application.} Applied to the iter-193 Stage~3
329	  setup --- \(k = \bar k\), \(S = \Gamma(X.\mathrm{left}, V)\) a
330	  standard-smooth \(\bar k\)-algebra of relative dimension \(n\) (Stage
331	  3), and \(\mathfrak q\) the prime defining the point \(z \in V\) --- the
332	  formula yields \(\dim(\mathcal O_{X, z}) = \dim S_{\mathfrak q} = n -
333	  \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(z)\). For \(z\) a closed point this is
334	  simply \(n\); for the generic point of a codim-\(1\) subvariety
335	  (\(\mathrm{trdeg} = n - 1\)) it is \(1\).
336	\end{lemma}
337	
338	\begin{lemma}
339	  [Stage 6.B --- Cotangent \(\leftrightarrow\) K\"ahler over a field (Stacks 02JK)]
340	  \label{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field}
341	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.cotangent_iso_residue_tensor_kaehler_of_formallySmooth_residue}
342	  % \lean{...} updated iter-199 review: iter-199 prover landed the
343	  % closed-point iso as a project-local axiom-clean helper (private
344	  % theorem in AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme namespace). Companion siblings:
345	  %   cotangent_iso_maximalIdeal_residue_tensor_kaehler_of_formallySmooth_residue
346	  %   finrank_cotangentSpace_of_formallySmooth_residue
347	  %   finrank_cotangentSpace_of_bijective_algebraMap_residue (closed-point bundled)
348	  \uses{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula}
349	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], lemma-differential-seq (canonically
350	  % known as the right-exact conormal portion of tag 02JK; the
351	  % short-exact / split refinement is the proof step inside
352	  % lemma-separable-smooth) (read from
353	  % references/stacks-algebra.tex, L34087--34104 and L38724--38766).
354	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "In diagram (\ref{equation-functorial-omega}),
355	  % suppose that $S \to S'$ is surjective with kernel $I \subset S$, and
356	  % assume that $R' = R$. Then there is a canonical exact sequence of
357	  % $S'$-modules
358	  % $$I/I^2 \longrightarrow \Omega_{S/R} \otimes_S S' \longrightarrow
359	  % \Omega_{S'/R} \longrightarrow 0$$
360	  % The leftmost map is characterized by the rule that $f \in I$ maps
361	  % to $\text{d}f \otimes 1$."
362	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Hence we get
363	  % $$\dim_{\kappa} \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa = \dim_\kappa
364	  % \mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2 + \text{trdeg}_k (\kappa) \geq \dim R +
365	  % \text{trdeg}_k (\kappa) = \dim_{\mathfrak q} S$$
366	  % (see Lemma \ref{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field} for
367	  % the last equality) with equality if and only if $R$ is regular."
368	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag~02JK (right-exact half = \texttt{lemma-differential-seq}; separable-residue refinement inside \texttt{lemma-separable-smooth}).}
369	  Let \(k\) be a field and let \(R\) be a Noetherian local
370	  \(k\)-algebra with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\) and residue field
371	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak m\). The canonical exact sequence
372	  \[
373	    \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2
374	    \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa
375	    \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{\kappa / k}
376	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
377	  \]
378	  identifies the cotangent space \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) (a
379	  \(\kappa\)-vector space) with the kernel of the right map. When
380	  \(\kappa / k\) is separable (in particular when \(k\) is
381	  algebraically closed and \(\kappa = k\), so \(\Omega_{\kappa / k}
382	  = 0\)) the leftmost map is injective and the sequence is short exact
383	  --- in fact split short exact when there is a \(k\)-algebra section
384	  \(\kappa \to R\) (e.g.\ at a closed point of a \(\bar k\)-variety).
385	  In particular, over an algebraically closed field \(k = \bar k\) and
386	  at a closed point \(\mathfrak m\),
387	  \[
388	    \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa \;\simeq\; \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2
389	  \]
390	  as \(\kappa\)-vector spaces; hence
391	  \(\dim_\kappa(\Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa)
392	   = \dim_\kappa \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\).
393	\end{lemma}
394	
395	\begin{lemma}
396	  [Stage 6.C --- Assembly: smooth standard-smooth stalk is regular local]
397	  \label{lem:stage6_regular_stalk_assembly}
398	  % NOTE (iter-199): the Lean target for this 6.C assembly is the body
399	  % of \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.isRegularLocalRing\_stalk\_of\_smooth}
400	  % itself (see \cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}); the iter-198
401	  % blueprint named a planned \texttt{...\_aux} extraction that has not
402	  % been created in the Lean file. Treat the lemma below as the in-body
403	  % closure pattern that the existing helper consumes once Stages 6.A and
404	  % 6.B land, rather than as a separately-pinned Lean declaration.
405	  \uses{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim,
406	        lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula,
407	        lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field}
408	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field, let
409	  \(R \to S\) be a standard-smooth \(\bar k\)-algebra presentation of
410	  relative dimension \(n\) on an affine chart \(\Spec S \subseteq X\),
411	  and let \(z \in \Spec S\) be a point corresponding to a prime
412	  \(\mathfrak q \subset S\). Then the stalk \(S_{\mathfrak q}\) is a
413	  regular local ring of Krull dimension
414	  \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\). In the
415	  closed-point case (\(\kappa(\mathfrak q) = \bar k\),
416	  \(\mathrm{trdeg} = 0\)) the dimension equals \(n\); in the
417	  codim-\(1\) generic-point case (\(\mathrm{trdeg} = n - 1\)) the
418	  dimension equals \(1\).
419	\end{lemma}
420	
421	\begin{proof}
422	  Combine the three sub-lemmas:
423	  \begin{enumerate}
424	    \item By \cref{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim} (Stage~5b)
425	      the localised K\"ahler module \(\Omega_{S_{\mathfrak q} / R}\)
426	      is free of rank \(n\) over \(S_{\mathfrak q}\).
427	    \item By \cref{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula} (Stage 6.A,
428	      Stacks 00OE) the Krull dimension of \(S_{\mathfrak q}\) equals
429	      \(\dim_z \Spec S - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\),
430	      which for a standard-smooth presentation of relative dimension
431	      \(n\) equals \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\).
432	      (For the standard-smooth presentation the dimension
433	      \(\dim_z \Spec S = n\) follows from the global-complete-intersection
434	      structure: \(n\) variables modulo \(n - \dim\) equations, see
435	      Stacks \texttt{lemma-relative-global-complete-intersection-smooth}.)
436	    \item By \cref{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field} (Stage 6.B,
437	      Stacks 02JK) the residue-field tensor \(\Omega_{S_{\mathfrak q}/R}
438	      \otimes_{S_{\mathfrak q}} \kappa(\mathfrak q)\) is isomorphic to
439	      \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) as \(\kappa(\mathfrak q)\)-vector
440	      spaces. Combined with (1), \(\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak
441	      m / \mathfrak m^2 = n\) at the closed-point case (and in general
442	      \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\) by base-change
443	      of the standard-smooth presentation to the closed-point fibre).
444	    \item Compare (2) and (3): both \(\dim S_{\mathfrak q}\) and
445	      \(\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) equal
446	      \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\); the
447	      regularity criterion for Noetherian local rings (the equality
448	      \(\dim_\kappa \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2 = \dim R\) is the
449	      defining condition for a regular local ring, Stacks
450	      \texttt{definition-regular-local} \(=\) Mathlib
451	      \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing}) then gives \(S_{\mathfrak q}\)
452	      regular. The scheme-level transfer
453	      \(S_{\mathfrak q} = \mathcal O_{X, z}\) (Stage 3
454	      \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime} on the affine chart) completes
455	      the conclusion.
456	  \end{enumerate}
457	\end{proof}

576	\subsection{Stage 6.A iter-200 substrate: Stacks 00OE 3-step decomposition}
577	\label{subsec:stage6_iib_substrate_iter200}
578	\textit{Source: \texttt{coe-stacks00oe} mathlib-analogist
579	cross-domain-inspiration recipe (iter-200, persistent
580	\texttt{analogies/coe-stacks00oe.md}); 7 axiom-clean
581	\texttt{private} substrate theorems landed in
582	\texttt{CodimOneExtension.lean} L688--L800 under
583	\texttt{namespace AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme}.}
584	
585	The iter-200 Lane COE prover phase decomposed Stage 6.A
586	(\ref{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula}, Stacks 00OE) into a
587	3-step Mathlib-chain. Steps 1 and 2 landed axiom-clean as
588	project-local \texttt{private} theorems; Step 3 landed in an
589	additive parameterised form awaiting the Jacobian-regular-sequence
590	witness for the relations of the smooth-algebra presentation.
591	
592	\paragraph{Step 1 --- Krull-dim \(\Rightarrow\) height (trivial,
593	\(\sim 1\) line).} Re-export of Mathlib's
594	\(\texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height}\) under
595	the project-local name
596	\(\texttt{ringKrullDim\_localization\_eq\_height\_atPrime}\); reduces
597	the localised Krull-dim goal to a height-of-maximal-ideal goal.
598	
599	\paragraph{Step 2 --- Polynomial-ring maximal-ideal height
600	(\(\sim 60\) LOC).} For \(\texttt{MvPolynomial}\,(\texttt{Fin}\,n)\,k\)
601	with \(k\) a field, every maximal ideal has height \emph{exactly}
602	\(n\). Lower bound:
603	\(\texttt{MvPolynomial.maximalIdeal\_height\_ge\_card\_of\_field}\),
604	induction on \texttt{Fin n} via \(\texttt{MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv}\)
605	+ Mathlib's \(\texttt{Polynomial.height\_eq\_height\_add\_one}\) plus
606	the Jacobson contraction
607	\(\texttt{Polynomial.isMaximal\_comap\_C\_of\_isJacobsonRing}\). Upper
608	bound:
609	\(\texttt{MvPolynomial.maximalIdeal\_height\_le\_natCard\_of\_field}\)
610	via \(\texttt{Ideal.height\_le\_ringKrullDim\_of\_ne\_top}\) +
611	\(\texttt{MvPolynomial.ringKrullDim\_of\_isNoetherianRing}\) +
612	\(\texttt{ringKrullDim\_eq\_zero\_of\_field}\). Combined: the
613	\texttt{Fin n}-form
614	\(\texttt{MvPolynomial.maximalIdeal\_height\_eq\_card}\) and the
615	general \texttt{[Finite ι]}-form
616	\(\texttt{MvPolynomial.maximalIdeal\_height\_eq\_natCard}\) via
617	\(\texttt{MvPolynomial.renameEquiv}\) +
618	\(\texttt{RingEquiv.height\_map}\). Capstone packaging the
619	Steps 1+2 composition for any localisation at a maximal ideal:
620	\(\texttt{ringKrullDim\_localization\_atMaximal\_MvPolynomial}\).
621	
622	\paragraph{Step 3 --- Drop by regular-sequence length (additive form
623	in hand, witness pending).} Step 3 generally:
624	\(\texttt{ringKrullDim}\,(R'_{m'} / (f_j)_{j \in \sigma}) +
625	 |\sigma| = \texttt{ringKrullDim}\,R'_{m'}\). The additive form is
626	landed as
627	\(\texttt{ringKrullDim\_quotient\_add\_eq\_of\_regular\_sequence}\)
628	via Mathlib's
629	\(\texttt{ringKrullDim\_add\_length\_eq\_ringKrullDim\_of\_isRegular}\),
630	parameterised over a hypothesis
631	\(\texttt{RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular}\,R'_{m'}\,\texttt{rs}\)
632	that is the iter-201+ residual substrate obligation. Note:
633	\(\texttt{WithBot}\,\mathbb N_\infty\) carries no \texttt{HSub}
634	instance, so the additive form is the natural API; a subtraction
635	form is not available.
636	
637	\paragraph{Iter-201+ Jacobian-regular-sequence witness recipe.} The
638	residual substrate of Stage 6.A is the Jacobian-criterion
639	regular-sequence witness for the relations of an
640	\(\texttt{Algebra.SubmersivePresentation}\).
641	
642	\textbf{API choice} (per iter-201 \texttt{progress-critic route201}
643	flag): the project-local target predicate is Mathlib's
644	\(\texttt{RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular}\), \emph{not}
645	\(\texttt{IsWeaklyRegular}\). Reason: the iter-200 substrate
646	\(\texttt{ringKrullDim\_quotient\_add\_eq\_of\_regular\_sequence}\)
647	(\texttt{CodimOneExtension.lean} L807) consumes the strong predicate
648	\(\texttt{IsRegular}\) (via Mathlib's
649	\(\texttt{ringKrullDim\_add\_length\_eq\_ringKrullDim\_of\_isRegular}\));
650	the localised stalk \(R'_{m'}\) is local, so by Mathlib's
651	\(\texttt{IsLocalRing.isRegular\_iff\_isWeaklyRegular\_of\_subset\_maximalIdeal}\)
652	the two predicates are equivalent under the standing hypothesis that
653	the relations lie in the maximal ideal --- which holds because
654	the Jacobian-criterion conclusion is precisely that the relations
655	generate (a subset of) the maximal ideal at the closed point. The
656	recipe builds \(\texttt{IsWeaklyRegular}\) first (it is the
657	naturally-arising predicate from the Koszul-complex argument) and
658	then promotes to \(\texttt{IsRegular}\) via the bridge lemma; the
659	final consumer call uses the \(\texttt{IsRegular}\) form.
660	
661	The recipe (per the iter-200 prover handoff + the
662	\texttt{coe-stacks00oe} analogist + iter-201 \texttt{coe-stacks00sw}
663	analogist):
664	(i) extract the explicit
665	\(\texttt{Algebra.SubmersivePresentation}\,\bar k\,
666	 \Gamma(X.\texttt{left}, V)\) from
667	\(\texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}\) (Stage 6 sub-gap
668	(i) substrate iter-198); (ii) cite Stacks 00SW / 00OW + Matsumura
669	Thm 14.2: the relations \((f_j)_{j \in \sigma}\) of a submersive
670	presentation form a Koszul-regular sequence at the closed point
671	because the Jacobian matrix is invertible modulo the maximal ideal
672	\(\Leftrightarrow\) the images of \(f_j\) in \(\mathfrak m_A /
673	\mathfrak m_A^2\) are \(\kappa\)-linearly independent
674	\(\Rightarrow\) (Matsumura 14.2 / Stacks 00NQ on a regular local
675	ring) the \(f_j\) form a regular sequence in \(A\); (iii) bundle
676	as a 3-step chain:
677	\begin{itemize}
678	  \item \textbf{A1 — Matsumura helper} (revised cost
679	    \(\sim 80\)--\(150\) LOC \emph{including cross-file imports}; the
680	    earlier \(\sim 30\)--\(50\) LOC estimate did not account for
681	    project-local Stacks 00NQ + RLR-preservation supply): on a regular
682	    local Noetherian ring \((A, \mathfrak m)\) of Krull dim \(n\), a
683	    sequence \(f_1, \dots, f_c \in \mathfrak m\) whose images in
684	    \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) are \(\kappa\)-linearly

975	\begin{proof}
976	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00TT.} The cited lemma packages the
977	  Jacobian-criterion equivalence and concludes ``in this case the local ring
978	  \(S_{\mathfrak q}\) is regular''. Translated to the scheme level: a smooth
979	  finite-type morphism \(X \to \Spec(\bar k)\) restricted to an affine chart
980	  \(\Spec(S) \subseteq X\) gives an affine algebra \(\bar k \to S\) that is
981	  smooth at every prime \(\mathfrak q \subset S\); tag 00TT then yields
982	  regularity of the localisation \(S_{\mathfrak q} = \mathcal O_{X, z}\) at every
983	  point \(z \in X\) lying over \(\mathfrak q\).
984	
985	  Mathlib hooks the prover will assemble for the formalised version of this
986	  block at commit \texttt{b80f227}: (i) \texttt{Algebra.FormallySmooth} and the
987	  smooth-locus-of-a-scheme typeclass instance threading
988	  \texttt{[Smooth\,X.hom]} down to the affine algebra
989	  \(\bar k \to \mathcal O_X(U)\) on each open affine \(U \subseteq X\); (ii) the
990	  Jacobian-criterion refinement of \texttt{Module.FormallyEtale} /
991	  \texttt{Algebra.Smooth} via the cotangent complex \texttt{H\^{}1\,LSm} (or
992	  equivalent), as encoded in Mathlib's \texttt{Algebra.Smooth.iff} family; (iii)
993	  the closed-point selection that, given a non-closed \(z\), picks a closed
994	  specialisation \(x \rightsquigarrow z\) whose stalk localises to
995	  \(\mathcal O_{X, z}\) (Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.localRing\_at\_closed\_point}
996	  family); (iv) preservation of regularity under localisation of a regular
997	  local ring at a prime (Mathlib
998	  \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing.localization\_isRegularLocalRing}, or the same
999	  fact phrased via Stacks tag \texttt{00OF}). The composition of (i)--(iv) is
1000	  the formalised content of the smooth-implies-regular implication; the only
1001	  Mathlib gap at \texttt{b80f227} is in step (ii)--(iii), specifically the
1002	  Jacobian-criterion scheme-level packaging that promotes
1003	  \texttt{[Smooth\,X.hom]\,[IsAlgClosed\,\bar k]} to
1004	  \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing\,(X.left.presheaf.stalk\,z)}.
1005	
1006	  As a backup citation, Matsumura, \emph{Commutative Ring Theory}, Chapter~19
1007	  (``Regular Rings'') develops the regular-local-ring theory needed for
1008	  step~(iv); see \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} for the depth-/CM-side
1009	  references the project already maintains.
1010	\end{proof}

1026	  % varieties over a field (I, \S5) and noetherian normal schemes. On a nonsingular
1027	  % variety the local ring of every closed point is regular (I, 5.1), hence all the

1033	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (definition of regular in codimension one;

1035	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\) and let \(\eta \in X\) be a point with
1036	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\). Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a
1037	  discrete valuation ring with fraction field the function field \(K(X)\) of \(X\).

1057	  every point \(x \in X\) --- in particular at every closed point (the case
1058	  Hartshorne~I.5.1 states classically). Localising a regular local ring at a
1059	  prime ideal yields a regular local ring; in particular, for \(\eta \in X\) with
1060	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\), the local ring
1061	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is regular of Krull dimension \(1\). A regular local ring of Krull dimension \(1\) is exactly a
1062	  discrete valuation ring (Atiyah--Macdonald, Proposition 9.2; or Stacks tag
1063	  \texttt{00PD}). Its fraction field is the function field \(K(X)\) of \(X\) by
1064	  irreducibility of \(X\) (the unique generic point of \(X\) lies above \(\eta\), and the
1065	  stalk at the generic point of an integral scheme is the function field).
1066	
1067	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
1068	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.localRing\_dvr\_of\_codim\_one} is the
1069	  instance/lemma stating that \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a Mathlib
1070	  \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing} under the typeclass set
1071	  \texttt{[Smooth\,(\dots)\,X.hom]\ [IsIntegral X.left]} and the hypothesis
1072	  \texttt{Order.coheight\ \(\eta\) = 1}. The fraction-field identification with
1073	  \texttt{X.functionField} is a downstream named instance
1074	  \texttt{Scheme.fractionRing\_localRing\_eq\_functionField} (or whatever Mathlib name
1075	  the prover chooses at commit \texttt{b80f227}). The regular-local-ring-of-dimension-1
1076	  \(\Leftrightarrow\) DVR equivalence is shipped in Mathlib as
1077	  \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing.isDiscreteValuationRing\_of\_dim\_one} (or analogous);
1078	  the prover re-exports it rather than re-proving.
1079	\end{proof}

1081	\section{Milne Theorem 3.1: codimension-\(\geq 2\) extension}
1082	\label{sec:milne_thm31}
1083	
1084	The first input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is the following: a rational map from a normal
1085	variety to a \emph{complete} variety automatically extends across every codim-\(1\)
1086	point.
1087	
1088	\begin{theorem}
1089	
1090	\leanok
1091	  [Milne Theorem~3.1: codim-\(\geq 2\) extension to a complete target]
1092	  \label{thm:codim_one_extension}
1093	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth}
1094	  
1095	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16
1096	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
1097	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Theorem 3.1.} \emph{A rational map $\varphi \colon V
1098	  % \dashrightarrow W\( from a normal variety \)V\( to a complete variety \)W$ is defined
1099	  % on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension
1100	  % \(\geq 2\).}"
1101	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16.}
1102	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a complete variety over
1103	  \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map. Then the
1104	  indeterminacy locus \(Z(f)\) (\cref{def:indeterminacy_locus}) has codimension
1105	  \(\geq 2\) in \(X\); equivalently, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
1106	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}).
1107	
1108	  Specialising further: if in addition \(f\) \emph{is} codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
1109	  ``for free'' (i.e.\ no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in \(Z(f)\)), then
1110	  \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) = X\) and \(f\) extends to a regular morphism $\widetilde f \colon X
1111	  \to Y\(. The extension is unique because \)X\( is reduced and \)\mathrm{Dom}(f)$ is dense
1112	  in \(X\).
1113	\end{theorem}
1114	
1115	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "\textsc{Proof.} Assume first that \(V\) is a curve. Thus we are
1116	% given a nonsingular curve \(V\) and a regular map \(\varphi \colon U \to W\) from an
1117	% open subset of \(V\) which we want to extend to \(V\). Consider the maps
1118	% [diagram: \(U \hookrightarrow V\), $U \xrightarrow{u \mapsto (u, \varphi(u))} Z
1119	% \subseteq V \times W\(, \)U \xrightarrow{\varphi} W$, with projections from
1120	% \(V \times W\) to \(V\) and to \(W\)]
1121	% Let \(U'\) be the image of \(U\) in \(V \times W\), and let \(Z\) be its closure. The image
1122	% of \(Z\) in \(V\) is closed (because \(W\) is complete), and contains \(U\) (the composite
1123	% \(U \to V\) is the given inclusion), and so \(Z\) maps onto \(V\). The maps $U \to U'
1124	% \to U\( are isomorphisms. Therefore, \)Z \to V$ is a surjective map from a complete
1125	% curve onto a nonsingular complete curve that is an isomorphism on open subsets.
1126	% Such a map must be an isomorphism (complete nonsingular curves are determined by
1127	% their function fields). The restriction of the projection map \(V \times W \to W\)
1128	% to \(Z\ (\simeq V)\) is the extension of \(\varphi\) to \(V\) we are seeking.
1129	%   The general case can be reduced to the one-dimensional case (using schemes). Let
1130	% \(U\) be the largest subset on which \(\varphi\) is defined, and suppose that $V
1131	% \smallsetminus U\( has codimension 1. Then there is a prime divisor \)Z\( in \)V
1132	% \smallsetminus U\(. Because \)V$ is normal, its associated local ring is a discrete
1133	% valuation ring \(\mathcal O_Z\) with field of fractions \(k(V)\). The map \(\varphi\)
1134	% defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(k(V)) \to W\), which the
1135	% valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne 1977, II 4.7) shows extends to a
1136	% morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_Z) \to W\). This implies that \(\varphi\)
1137	% has a representative defined on an open subset that meets \(Z\) in a nonempty set,
1138	% which is a contradiction."
1139	\begin{proof}
1140	  
1141	  Following Milne's two-step proof.
1142	
1143	  \emph{Step 1: rule out codim-\(1\) components.} Suppose for contradiction that \(Z(f)\)
1144	  contains a prime divisor \(W \subseteq X\) (i.e.\ a codim-\(1\) irreducible component).
1145	  Let \(\eta \in W\) be its generic point, so \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\). By
1146	  \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a discrete
1147	  valuation ring with fraction field the function field \(K(X)\). Any representative
1148	  \((U, \varphi_U) \in f\) has \(U \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f) \neq \emptyset\) and so determines
1149	  a \(\bar k\)-algebra map from the function field \(K(Y)\) of \(Y\) to \(K(X)\): explicitly,
1150	  \(\varphi_U\) defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X)) \to Y\) (since
1151	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X))\) is the generic point of \(X\)). By the valuative criterion
1152	  of properness applied to the complete variety \(Y\)
1153	  (\emph{cf.}\ Hartshorne~II.4.7, cited verbatim in Milne~3.1's proof), this morphism
1154	  extends to a morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}) \to Y\). Standard
1155	  algebraic-geometry spreading then exhibits an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(\eta\) in
1156	  \(X\) on which \(f\) has a regular representative; in particular $\eta \in
1157	  \mathrm{Dom}(f)\(, contradicting \)\eta \in W \subseteq Z(f)\(. Hence no codim-\)1$
1158	  component of \(X\) lies in \(Z(f)\), i.e.\ \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\).
1159	
1160	  \emph{Step 2: extend across codim-\(\geq 2\) points (the "codim-1-indeterminacy-free
1161	  \(\Rightarrow\) extension" half).} Suppose now that \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)
1162	  but is non-empty (we want to deduce \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) under the additional input
1163	  that ``\(f\) is already codim-1-indeterminacy-free, by hypothesis''). Pick a closed
1164	  point \(x \in Z(f)\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} \geq 2\). By
1165	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} applied to the regular local ring
1166	  \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\), we have
1167	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = \dim(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2\). The sheaf
1168	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on the punctured neighbourhood \(V \smallsetminus \{x\}\)
1169	  (any small affine neighbourhood \(V \ni x\)) has \(H^0_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\)
1170	  and \(H^1_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\) (the depth-\(\geq 2\) characterisation of
1171	  local-cohomology vanishing, Stacks tag \texttt{0AVF}; cf.\ Hartshorne~III.3.5 for
1172	  the surface case). Pull back the coordinates of any affine chart of \(Y\) around the
1173	  closure \(\overline{f(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \cap V)}\) along \(f\); the resulting sections of
1174	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on \(V \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) extend uniquely to sections on \(V\),
1175	  yielding a regular representative of \(f\) on \(V\). Hence \(x \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\),
1176	  contradicting \(x \in Z(f)\). Therefore \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) and \(f\) extends to a
1177	  regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to Y\).
1178	
1179	  Uniqueness of \(\widetilde f\) is the standard reduced-and-separated agreement
1180	  principle: two morphisms \(X \to Y\) that agree on the dense open \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\)
1181	  agree on all of \(X\).
1182	\end{proof}
1183	
1184	\begin{remark}
1185	  \label{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}
1186	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} Step~1 of the proof uses only normality
1187	  + the valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne~II.4.7) and is characteristic-free.
1188	  Step~2 uses the depth-\(\geq 2\) extension property at a codim-\(\geq 2\) regular
1189	  point, which is exactly what \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of
1190	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies (regular local of dimension \(d\)
1191	  \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay of depth \(d\)). For our application the source \(X\) is a
1192	  smooth variety, so the regular-local-ring hypothesis is automatic; the Cohen--Macaulay
1193	  consequence is what feeds the local-cohomology vanishing.
1194	
1195	  Concretely, when \(X\) is a smooth surface (e.g.\ \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) or
1196	  \(C \times C\) for a smooth projective curve \(C\)), the codim-\(\geq 2\) closed points of
1197	  \(X\) are exactly the closed points of \(X\) (because \(\dim X = 2\)), and at each such
1198	  point \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is regular of dimension \(2\), hence Cohen--Macaulay of
1199	  depth \(2\). The smooth-surface case is the one A.4.c will invoke when extending the
1200	  symmetric-product rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) along the Abel--Jacobi
1201	  birational equivalence to \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\).
1202	\end{remark}

1204	\section{Milne Lemma 3.3: indeterminacy locus of a map to a group variety is codim 1}
1205	\label{sec:milne_lem33}
1206	
1207	The second input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is structural: when the target \(Y\) is a group
1208	variety, the indeterminacy locus is either empty or of \emph{pure} codimension \(1\).
1209	Combined with \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s ``codimension \(\geq 2\)'' conclusion,
1210	this forces the locus to be empty.
1211	
1212	\begin{lemma}
1213	
1214	\leanok
1215	  [Milne Lemma~3.3: pure-codim-\(1\) structure of the indeterminacy locus into a group
1216	  variety]
1217	  \label{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}
1218	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacy_pure_codim_one_into_grpScheme}
1219	  
1220	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Lemma~3.3, \S I.3, p.~17
1221	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
1222	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Lemma 3.3.} \emph{Let \(\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow G\)
1223	  % be a rational map from a nonsingular variety to a group variety. Then either
1224	  % \(\varphi\) is defined on all of \(V\) or the points where it is not defined form a
1225	  % closed subset of pure codimension 1 in \(V\) (i.e., a finite union of prime
1226	  % divisors).}"
1227	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Lemma~3.3, \S I.3, p.~17.}
1228	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(G\) be a group variety over
1229	  \(\bar k\) (a smooth group scheme of finite type), and let $f \colon X \dashrightarrow
1230	  G\( be a rational map. Then either \)f\( is defined on all of \)X$, or the indeterminacy
1231	  locus \(Z(f) \subseteq X\) is a closed subset of pure codimension \(1\) (i.e.\ a finite
1232	  union of prime divisors).
1233	
1234	  Equivalently: it is \emph{never} the case that \(f\) has a non-empty indeterminacy
1235	  locus all of whose irreducible components have codimension \(\geq 2\).
1236	\end{lemma}

1273	  
1274	  Following Milne's proof of Lemma~3.3. The argument has four sub-steps. Throughout we
1275	  fix a representative \((U, \varphi_U)\) of \(f\).
1276	
1277	  \emph{Sub-step 1: the difference rational map.} Define
1278	  \[
1279	    \Phi \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow G,
1280	    \qquad
1281	    \Phi(x, y) \;:=\; \varphi(x) \cdot \varphi(y)^{-1},
1282	  \]
1283	  via the composition
1284	  \[
1285	    U \times U
1286	    \;\xrightarrow{\varphi_U \times \varphi_U}\; G \times G
1287	    \;\xrightarrow{\mathrm{id} \times \mathrm{inv}}\; G \times G
1288	    \;\xrightarrow{m}\; G,
1289	  \]
1290	  where \(\mathrm{inv} \colon G \to G\) is the group-variety inverse and
1291	  \(m \colon G \times G \to G\) is the group law. The map \(\Phi\) is rational by
1292	  composition: its domain is at least \(U \times U \subseteq X \times X\), which is dense
1293	  because \(U\) is dense in \(X\).
1294	
1295	  \emph{Sub-step 2: \(\Phi\)-defined at \((x, x)\) \(\Leftrightarrow\) \(\varphi\)-defined at
1296	  \(x\).} If \(\varphi\) is defined at \(x\), the explicit formula \(\Phi(x, x) = e\) (the
1297	  identity of \(G\)) shows \(\Phi\) is defined at \((x, x)\). Conversely, if \(\Phi\) is
1298	  defined at \((x, x)\), openness of \(\mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)\) in \(X \times X\) produces an
1299	  open set \(W \ni (x, x)\) on which \(\Phi\) is defined; by projection (taking the slice
1300	  \(\{x\} \times W' \subseteq W\) for some open \(W' \subseteq X\), possibly shrinking
1301	  \(W'\) so \(\varphi\) is defined on \(W'\) as well --- \(W'\) need not contain \(x\)), the
1302	  identity
1303	  \[
1304	    \varphi(x) \;=\; \Phi(x, u) \cdot \varphi(u),
1305	    \qquad u \in W',
1306	  \]
1307	  exhibits a regular representative of \(\varphi\) at \(x\). Hence $x \in
1308	  \mathrm{Dom}(\varphi)\( if and only if \)(x, x) \in \mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)$.
1309	
1310	  \emph{Sub-step 3: rephrase via pullback of local rings.} The rational map \(\Phi\)
1311	  pulls \(\mathcal O_{G, e}\) back to a \(\bar k\)-subalgebra of the function field
1312	  \(K(X \times X)\):
1313	  \[
1314	    \Phi^\ast \colon \mathcal O_{G, e} \;\longrightarrow\; K(X \times X).
1315	  \]
1316	  Because \(\Phi\) sends \((x, x)\) to \(e\) wherever it is defined, \(\Phi\) is defined at
1317	  \((x, x)\) if and only if $\Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e}) \subseteq \mathcal O_{X
1318	  \times X, (x, x)}\( (i.e.\ every local function on \)G\( near \)e$ has no pole at
1319	  \((x, x)\) after pullback).
1320	
1321	  \emph{Sub-step 4: pole locus is pure codimension \(1\).} The variety \(X \times X\) is
1322	  nonsingular (smoothness is stable under products over \(\bar k\)), so the Weil-divisor
1323	  apparatus of \cref{def:codim1_cycles} and the order map of
1324	  \cref{def:order_at_point} apply on \(X \times X\). For any nonzero rational function
1325	  \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) decompose its divisor as
1326	  \[
1327	    \mathrm{div}(f) \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f)_0 - \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty,
1328	  \]
1329	  with \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) effective and
1330	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = \mathrm{div}(f^{-1})_0\) (Milne's notation, matching the
1331	  project's \(\div(f) = \sum \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y\) of
1332	  \cref{def:principal_divisor}: \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) collects the prime divisors with
1333	  \(\ord_Y(f) > 0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) those with \(\ord_Y(f) < 0\)). The local
1334	  ring at \((x, x)\) can be characterised as
1335	  \[
1336	    \mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}
1337	    \;=\; \bigl\{\, f \in K(X \times X) \;\big|\;
1338	    \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x) \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
1339	  \]
1340	  Suppose \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) is not in \(\mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}\);
1341	  equivalently, \((x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty)\). The pole divisor
1342	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) is a finite sum of prime divisors of \(X \times X\) (i.e.\ a
1343	  closed subset of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X \times X\)). Its intersection with the
1344	  diagonal \(\Delta_X \subseteq X \times X\) is a closed subset of pure codimension \(1\)
1345	  in \(\Delta_X\) (this is Milne's reference to AG~9.2, the standard fact that a prime
1346	  divisor of \(X \times X\) either contains \(\Delta_X\) or meets it in pure codimension
1347	  \(1\); on a nonsingular variety this is an instance of Krull's principal-ideal theorem
1348	  applied to the local equation of \(\Delta_X\) inside \(X \times X\)).
1349	
1350	  \emph{Conclusion.} Suppose \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow G\) is not defined on all of
1351	  \(X\). Pick \(x \in Z(f)\). By Sub-steps~2--3, there is some $f_0 \in
1352	  \Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e})\( with \)(x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty)$.
1353	  By Sub-step~4 the intersection $\mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty) \cap
1354	  \Delta_X\( is pure codimension \)1\( in \)\Delta_X\(, passing through \)(x, x)$.
1355	  Identifying \(\Delta_X\) with \(X\) via the diagonal isomorphism, the corresponding
1356	  closed subset of \(X\) has pure codimension \(1\) and passes through \(x\). By Sub-step~2,
1357	  every point of this codim-\(1\) subset is a point where \(\Phi\) is undefined on the
1358	  diagonal, hence where \(\varphi\) is undefined. Thus \(Z(\varphi)\) contains a pure
1359	  codim-\(1\) closed subset passing through \(x\); since \(x \in Z(\varphi)\) was arbitrary,
1360	  every irreducible component of \(Z(\varphi)\) has codimension exactly \(1\), i.e.\
1361	  \(Z(\varphi)\) is of pure codimension \(1\).
1362	\end{proof}
1363	
1364	\begin{remark}
1365	  \label{rmk:milne_lem33_charfree}
1366	  \textbf{Characteristic-free.} The above proof uses only: (i) the group law on \(G\)
1367	  and its inverse (formal smoothness of \(G\) as a group object); (ii) the Weil-divisor
1368	  apparatus on the nonsingular variety \(X \times X\) (Hartshorne~II.6, or
1369	  \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor} in the project's notation); (iii) the
1370	  decomposition of a divisor on a nonsingular variety into effective pieces, and
1371	  Krull's principal-ideal theorem on \(X \times X\). None of these has a characteristic
1372	  restriction, so \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} holds over every
1373	  algebraically closed base field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states the
1374	  lemma in this generality at \S I.3, p.~17.)
1375	\end{remark}
